musicalLanguage.description: "November Rain" (1991) is Guns N' Roses' sprawling 171-measure piano epic, and its harmony sits well outside its stated C major: the harness's fit score for C major is -0.041, essentially no correlation, while the confidence-gated windows spell a clean, strongly diatonic B major instead — B itself accounts for 26% of windows considered, the most dominant single label anywhere in this piece, alongside C#m (ii, 5%), E (IV, 4%), G#m7 (vi7, 4%), and F# (V, 3%). This annotation follows that B-major content rather than the stated key. At a moderate 80 BPM in 4/4, the piece is also structured around a single dramatic pause: measure 133 falls completely silent (both hands), and the section lens finds its two largest structural pivots anywhere in the piece immediately afterward (measures 133-141 and 142-149) — the harness's own fingerprint for this song's famous mid-piece shift into its extended climactic final section.
musicalLanguage.structure: The B-major verse material (measures 1-124) is built from a recurring 4-measure-interval cell (identical measures at 26, 30, 34, 38, 42, 54, 58, 120). A brief but complete silence at measure 133 (both hands tacet) marks the transition into the piece's most structurally novel territory (measures 133-149). The closing section (measures 150-171) sustains the piece's highest density (30 onsets in measures 150, 151, 160, 161, and 165) over a strong, repeating 42-interval left-hand figure (measures 150-154, 155-159, 160-164, 165-169) that drives the climax to its end.
keyMoments: The harness's fit score for the stated C major is -0.041 — essentially no correlation — while B accounts for 26% of windows considered, the most dominant single harmonic label in the piece. This annotation is grounded in that B-major content, not the config's C major.

Measure 133 falls completely silent in both hands — a deliberate written pause — immediately followed by the two largest structural novelty spikes anywhere in the piece (measures 133-141 and 142-149), the harness's own evidence for this song's famous shift into its extended final section.

The closing measures (150, 151, 160, 161, 165) all reach exactly 30 onsets, the densest sustained plateau in the piece, and they sit directly on top of a strong, repeating 42-interval left-hand figure — density and structure agreeing on where the climax lives.

A 4-measure-interval melodic cell recurs eight times across the verse (measures 26, 30, 34, 38, 42, 54, 58, 120) — a long-range structural thread tying the piece's first two-thirds together before the climactic final section breaks from it.
teachingGoals: Practice checking a piece's stated key against its actual harmonic content — this piece's C major label fits its own content at -0.041, while the B-major reading this annotation uses is strongly, cleanly supported at 26% coverage.
Learn to use a written silence (measure 133) as a real structural marker, not a page-turn — the piece's own biggest formal shift happens immediately after it.

Build toward the climactic final section (measures 150-171) knowing it is both the densest (30-onset measures) and the most repetitively driven (a strong 3.66-compression left-hand ostinato) part of the piece — technical endurance and rhythmic consistency both matter most here.

Recognize a long-range recurring cell (measures 26, 30, 34, 38, 42, 54, 58, 120) as a structural anchor threading through the verse — naming it makes an otherwise very long form easier to hold in memory.
styleTips: Listen for B major resolving with real finality, even though the stated key claims C — trust the confidence-gated evidence (26% of windows considered) over the config field.
Play measure 133's silence as a complete, committed stop — the structural pivot that follows depends on that pause actually registering as empty space first.

Feel the climactic left-hand ostinato (measures 150-169) as the engine driving the ending, not just an accompaniment — its own internal repetition is what gives the final section its sense of inevitability.
Try tracking the recurring 4-measure cell (measures 26 onward) consciously during practice — recognizing it by ear turns a 171-measure piece into a much shorter set of ideas to actually learn.
